rulings.Case Title: MARK EMMETT WILSON VS. MIGUEL ALEJANDRO RESCALVO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing Date On Moton For New Trial/Vacate Order [Ccp 657]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, May 26, 2015, Line 5, PLAINTIFF MARK WILSON'S Moton For New Trial/Vacate Order [CCP 657]. The motion for new trial is denied. DiGiacinto v. Ameriko-Omserv Corp. (1997) 59 Cal.App.4th 629, does not apply to this case because defendant prescribed a particular mode of acceptance and plaintiff did not accept the addendum in that manner. Defendant shifted the burden of production. Defendant's UMF 20 presented evidence showing that plaintiff refused to sign the addendum. (Rescalvo depo., pg. 123). In opposition, plaintiff failed to show proper acceptance and admitted that the addendum was kept at his desk. (Plaintiff's depo, pgs. 190 and 192). The court rejects all the cited grounds for a new trial. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: COX, CASTLE & NICHOLSON et al VS. ACCO ENGINEERED SYSTEMS, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Joint Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, May 26, 2015, Line 6, CROSS COMPLAINANT ACCO ENGINEERED SYSTEMS, INC.'S Motion And Joint Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement. The motion for good faith settlement is denied. The parties appear to agree that COX, BANA, or HWA (owner and tenants) are properly viewed as the "plaintiffs" in this case. With the assignment to ACCO, it is unclear what the value of these claims are and what credit AB&R is entitled to with regard to each claim per CCP 877. ACCO presents the Seyforth declaration, but he merely states in a conclusory manner that the assignments are not very valuable and were merely thrown in to help settle the matter. ACCO's settlement raises the same problems that existed in Alcal Roofing and Insulation v. Sup. Crt. (1992) 8 Cal.App.4th 1121, 1128. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday May 26, 2015, Line 1, Plaintiff D.L. Falk Construction Inc.'s Motion To Quash Defendants Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records To Y.A. Tittle & Associates And Asero Insurance Services. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to quash is denied as moot. The parties filed a formal stipulation on May 18, 2015 withdrawing the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JULIE FAUSTINO VS. PG&E CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of Pg&E And Lina Binns (Joinder)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, May 26, 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANT PAMELA MANICK'S Joinder in Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Of PG&E And Lina Binns. Motion for summary judgment denied. Motion for summary adjudication granted in part and denied in part. Granted as to the harassment cause of action. The evidence establishes that Defendant's alleged conduct was not "severe" or "pervasive." (Undisputed Material Fact ("UMF") 11-12.) Further, there is no evidence presented to raise a triable issue of material fact as to whether there is a nexus between the alleged harassing conduct and Plaintiff's age. (See Crawford v. Medina General Hospital (1996) 96 F.3d 830, 836.) It is undisputed that Plaintiff told the Corporate Security investigator that Manick had not harassed her on the basis of age. (UMF 26.) Granted as to the intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action. There is no evidence that any of Defendant's actions went beyond "mere insults, indignities, threats, annoyances, petty oppressions, or other trivialities." (Alcorn v. Anbro Eng., Inc. (1970) 2 Cal.3d 493, 499.) Denied as to the defamation per quod and the defamation per se causes of action. Defendant met her initial burden by introducing evidence showing her statement was one of opinion. (UMF 23-26.) Plaintiff raises a triable issue of material fact as to whether Defendant knew that Plaintiff had not been fired at the time the statement was made. (Plaintiff's response to UMF 23.) The Court did not consider Defendant's argument about Plaintiff being unable to establish damages because it was raised for the first time on reply. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: JULIE FAUSTINO VS. PG&E CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, May 26, 2015, Line 7, DEFENDANTS LINA BINNS, AND PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. (Part 1 of 2) Motion for summary judgment granted. As to the discrimination claim, Defendants have met their initial burden of showing that they had a legitimate, nondiscriminatory reason for disciplining Plaintiff, including placing Plaintiff on Decision Making Leave. (Defendants' Undisputed Material Facts ("UMF") 6, 8, 10-12.) Plaintiff has not met her burden of producing "substantial responsive evidence that the employer's showing was untrue or pretextual," thereby raising at least an inference of discrimination. (Hersant v. Department of Social Services (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 997, 1004-05.) A plaintiff's "suspicions of improper motives¿primarily based on conjecture and speculation" are not sufficient to raise a triable issue of fact to withstand summary judgment." (Kerr v. Rose (1990) 216 Cal.App.3d 1551, 1564.) The vast majority of Plaintiff's evidence is based on conjecture and speculation. Plaintiff's evidence only sets forth two facts that support her defamation claim. The first is that a former supervisor told Plaintiff that a customer complained about Plaintiff's age. (Faustino Decl., par. 19.) This statement does not evidence discrimination, as it was a customer, not a decisionmaker, who commented on Plaintiff's age. (See King v. United Parcel Service, Inc. (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 426, 433-34.) The second is Lina Binns asking Plaintiff if she was going to retire. (Faustino Decl., par. 21.) "Stray remarks are not sufficient to establish liability under the FEHA." (Harris v. City of Santa Monica (2013) 56 Cal.4th 203, 225.) Further, the undisputed evidence establishes that Plaintiff engaged in the conduct that she was disciplined for and that Plaintiff knew she could be disciplined for such conduct. (UMF 4, 6, 8, 11, 12.) Accordingly, summary adjudication is granted as to this cause of action. As to the harassment claim, Defendants met their initial burden showing that Plaintiff cannot establish all the elements. (UMF 1-16, 19-22.) Plaintiff has not raised a triable issue of material fact. Plaintiff does not dispute that the only allegedly harassing conduct on the part of Lina Binns were personnel management decisions. (UMF 21; See Janken v. GM Hughes Elec. (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 55.) Additionally, Plaintiff has not introduced evidence showing that any alleged harassment by Pamela Manick was based on her age. It is undisputed that Plaintiff told the Corporate Security investigator that Manick had not harassed her on the basis of age. (UMF 26.) Summary adjudication is granted as to this cause of action. Summary adjudication is granted as to the causes of action for failure to prevent discrimination and violation of Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 17200 because they are wholly derivative of the harassment and discrimination claims. Summary adjudication is granted as to the Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress claim. Based on the totality of the evidence presented, no trier of fact could find that the Defendants engaged in extreme or outrageous conduct. Lastly, summary adjudication is granted as to the Wrongful Constructive Discharge claim. Where a plaintiff fails to demonstrate the severe or pervasive harassment necessary to support a hostile work environment claim, it will be impossible for her to meet the higher standard of constructive discharge: conditions so intolerable that a reasonable person would leave the job." (Brooks v. City of San Mateo (9th Cir. 2000) 229 F.3d 917, 930.) (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: DINA SERGEYEVA VS. HEMAS, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Dina Sergeyeva'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two; Request For Admissions, Set Two And Three; And Motion To Impose Sanctions (3 Of 3)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, May 26, 2015, Line 13, Plaintiff Dina Sergeyeva'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two; Request For Admissions, Set Two And Three; And Motion To Impose Sanctions (Part 3 Of 3). #31-#32: These responses are sufficient as they are not aware of any such documents. (Code of Civ. Proc. §2031.010.) #33-#34: To the extent that such documents exist and are within defendant's custody or control, defendant shall produce or allow plaintiffs to inspect all non-privileged documents within the requested categories. Sanctions: Sanctions are mandated against the losing party unless that party made or opposed the motion with substantial justification or other circumstances make sanctions unjust. (Code of Civ. Proc. §§ 2031.310(d) [requests for productions], 2033.290(d).) Sanctions are awarded in the amount of $1,840.00 against defendant and their counsel. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: DINA SERGEYEVA VS. HEMAS, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Dina Sergeyeva'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two; Request For Admissions, Set Two And Three; And Motion To Impose Sanctions (Part 2 Of 3)
rulings.Rulings: Plaintiff Dina Sergeyeva'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two; Request For Admissions, Set Two And Three; And Motion To Impose Sanctions (Part 2 Of 3) Requests for Admission Set Three: #1-#8: These requests are relevant to the subject matter and need to be admitted, admitted in part, or denied. (Code of Civ. Proc. §2033.220.) #9-#10: These requests exceed the 35 allowable requests pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure §2033.050 and therefore need not be answered. Requests for Production of Documents: #1-#15, #19-#20, #24-28 : To the extent that such documents exist and are within defendant's custody or control, defendant shall produce or allow plaintiffs to inspect all non-privileged documents within the requested categories. #16-#18, #21-#23: The court assumes these are communications between defendant and contractors who performed the work which is the subject of the two permits for seismic work that plaintiffs contend may have damaged the sidewalk and the request is limited to communication between defendant and the respective contractor with respect to that work only. To the extent within defendant's custody or control, defendant shall produce or allow plaintiffs to inspect all non-privileged documents within the requested categories. #29-#30: To the extent any other non-privileged documents, beside the two which were produced, exist that are within defendant's custody or control they shall be produced. Otherwise, defendant shall indicate that there are no other documents within these two categories. (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 3 of 3) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: DINA SERGEYEVA VS. HEMAS, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two; Request For Admissions, Set Two And Three; And Motion To Impose Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, May 26, 2015, Line 15, PLAINTIFF DINA SERGEYEVA'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set Two; Request For Admissions, Set Two And Three; And Motion To Impose Sanctions. (Part 1 of 3) Requests for Admission Set Two: #2 and #4: These requests are relevant to the subject matter and need to be admitted, admitted in part, or denied. (Code of Civ. Proc. §2033.220.) In so far as the claim that the word "defect" is vague, defendants responded to #3 which contained similar issues. Therefore, defendant must respond to these requests, notwithstanding the objections so that plaintiffs know what facts are at issue and whether if there is a denial plaintiffs may be entitled to attorney fees pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure §2033.420(b) if they prove that matter at trial. Finally, ambiguity is not a proper objection unless the responding party cannot in good faith frame an intelligent response. (Cembrook v. Sup. Ct. (1961) 56 Cal.2d 423, 428.) #6, #7, and #8: These requests are relevant to the subject matter and need to be admitted, admitted in part, or denied. (Code of Civ. Proc. §2033.220.) The response that a document exists does not satisfy the need for a required response. (See AFSCME v. Metro. Water Dist. of So. Cal. (2005) 126 Cal.App.4th 247, 268.) #10, 11, 12, and 13: These requests are relevant to the subject matter and do not require premature disclosure given that trial is in approximately one month. No disclosure of privileged or expert information is required to respond and such requests are available to establish opinions, any matter in controversy, or legal conclusions. (Code of Civ. Proc. §3022.010.) #14: This request is very vague as to what is a "general legal counsel helping you with legal matters concerning your business" and is not relevant to the subject matter as it is overbroad and therefore the request need not be answered even though it does not call for any communication that would be privileged under the attorney client privilege. (Evid. Code §952; see Brunner v. Sup. Ct. (1959) 51 Cal.2d 616, 618.) (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 2 of 3) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: MIDLAND FUNDING LLC VS. KAREN PEREZ et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Establishing Admissions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday May 26, 2015, Line 4, PLAINTIFF MIDLAND FUNDING LLC'S Motion For Order Establishing Admissions. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion for an Order establishing admissions is granted without opposition. Moving party shall serve the Order on the attorney for the defendant with Notice of Entry. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
